If you don't want to keep it, bring it back. We give you all your money back. Not we give you another car. Not we, no, no, no. Here's your money. So everybody in the car business would have told you right up to this day that you can't make that math work. It's a horrible thing to do. People will take advantage. They will have 512 reasons why you can't do it. Within another six months, you'll see somebody else doing it. And probably within two years, it'll be the accepted norm in the industry. Because you'll be at such a competitive disadvantage, not doing it, that it'll become mandatory. Iacocca changed the whole automobile warranty industry and he was the only one. And then everybody caught up today since they're making such crappy cars. It's a real problem. Well, yesterday, Ford recalled $700,000. Toyota recalled $800,000 in the same day. Wow. But anyway, so again, there's hardly any business. So health professionals will tell you, we can't guarantee outcomes. But they can guarantee something in their business. Well, you can guarantee service. You can guarantee service. Exactly right. You can at least guarantee waiting time. That's right. You can say, right? You can guarantee. I mean, you can do dominoes. You can certainly do if we don't see you within 30 minutes. You can guarantee satisfaction if after your first four treatments, if you want to refund them up. So there's nothing you can't issue a guarantee in of some kind that puts a risk reversal element into your offer. Nick, there was a question for you actually, which isn't up there now. But because I have this mind of a steel sieve. Steel strap. Steel sieve. Very good. The question was about the person wanted you to talk a little bit more about romancing. Sure. You mentioned that earlier as far as the presentation. You've got to, during the presentation, you need to romance your product and your benefits and all that. Can you give an example of that? Of course. I think one of the things, people love the buy. And they love the showmanship of the buy. And let's use. They do. I mean, they use one of the most commoditized businesses out there in toilet paper. I befriended an older owner of a toilet paper. There's a theme going on here with us. But he, it's about as commoditized as they come. It's about as unsexy as they come. But his position in selling was, I'm going to know everything I possibly can about you as my prospect, as my customer. You're never going to have to worry about your inventory. I'm going to come in or send people in on a weekly basis and do the inventory. We're going to have it spot ordered. Halloween. Five kids. You're going to have five of the largest happy apples you're ever going to see. You remember it every Christmas, every holiday. And so he romanced the sale and then romanced the post sale over the course of time. And it was about as unsexy as a business as they come. So trying to use a very unsexy business. By the way, so there's a business selling to a retailer, a product that is good at command shelf space. Most people would argue that the retailer is going to buy the product that has the highest margin or has the lowest cost or the biggest brand name that is doing the most advertising. And there is no way to compete successfully for shelf space with those factors. In B2B, you will hear competitive bids. And there's nothing we can do about it. And now they make us email in our bids. So we email our proposal. And there's nothing we can do. And the buyer's good to buy. And I've proved it false again and again and again. It's just not true. And a lot of this, if you can't romance the product, which to his point, you really can't, right? You can romance the relationship. Or you can romance the value added that you bring to the product. Fantastic. Fantastic. I thought you were going with the toilet paper. That's just going to be a Dr. Scholls thing. And then they go into the store and sit on something and analyzes it.
